justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Four bounded contexts clearly identified — Route Planning, Fleet Management, Delivery Management, Customer Notification. (2) Cross-context dependencies explicitly called out in escalations and edge cases, particularly the route recalculation dependency on delivery lifecycle state changes and the vehicle class constraint spanning Fleet and Delivery contexts. (3) Vehicle class rule correctly marked invariant: true (BR-1), stop duration correctly marked invariant: false (BR-8). (4) Nine Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided, well exceeding the minimum of 4. (5) All three route recalculation triggers enumerated (new delivery, failed delivery, driver deviation) in BR-4/5/6.

observations.text: Four bounded contexts identified with clear ownership boundaries — exceeds the minimum of 3
Excellent invariant classification across all 9 business rules with detailed justifications
Nine acceptance criteria covering vehicle class filtering, all recalculation triggers, notifications, stop duration overrides, and duplicate notification prevention
Cross-context dependencies surfaced as formal escalations with options and recommendations — goes beyond just identifying them
Rich edge case coverage (10 scenarios) including concurrent recalculation, empty routes, zone reassignment, and GPS noise — demonstrates deep domain thinking
Multi-driver zone partitioning gap correctly identified as the highest-risk ambiguity — this is a sophisticated observation

Route recalculation triggers (BR-4/5/6) marked invariant: false — the rubric expected invariant: true since the system must always recalculate on these events. Bird's justification (they're optimization policy, not data integrity) is defensible but diverges from the expected classification
Business impact analysis is comprehensive with specific stakeholder-level impacts and risk prioritization

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Four bounded contexts clearly identified — Route Planning, Fleet Management, Delivery Management, Customer Notification. (2) Cross-context dependencies explicitly called out — Route Planning must respect Fleet Management constraints and consume Delivery Management events; escalations formally surface these as integration points for MJ. (3) Vehicle class rule correctly marked invariant: true (BR-1, BR-10), stop time correctly marked invariant: false (BR-6), notification window correctly marked invariant: false (BR-5). (4) Ten Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided, far exceeding the 4 minimum. (5) All three route recalculation triggers enumerated (new delivery BR-2, failed delivery BR-3, driver deviation BR-4).

observations.text: Four bounded contexts with clear ownership boundaries and interaction patterns described
Ten business rules with detailed invariant justifications — notably BR-9 (completed stops immutable) and BR-10 (enforcement at both assignment and recalculation) go beyond the rubric expectations
Ten acceptance criteria covering happy path, edge cases, and cross-context scenarios
Ten edge cases identified including recalculation storms, overlapping zones, and notification timing — shows deep domain reasoning
Four well-structured escalations with options and recommendations, surfacing genuine ambiguities for product clarification
Rich ubiquitous language glossary with 12 terms precisely defined

Route recalculation triggers (BR-2, BR-3, BR-4) marked invariant: false — the rubric expected invariant: true since the system must always recalculate on these events. Bird's justification (delayed recalculation doesn't corrupt data) is defensible but diverges from the expected classification
Business impact analysis covers financial, operational, user, and risk dimensions with specific stakeholder mapping
Confidence level of 62 with clearly delineated high/low confidence areas and explicit assumptions — shows intellectual honesty about spec gaps

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: 4 bounded contexts identified (Route Planning, Delivery Management, Fleet Management, Customer Notification). Cross-context dependencies explicitly identified in escalations and edge cases (e.g., route recalculation depending on delivery lifecycle state changes, vehicle class spanning Fleet and Route contexts). Vehicle class rule correctly marked invariant:true (BR-2), stop time correctly marked invariant:false (BR-8), notification window correctly marked invariant:false (BR-7). 10 acceptance criteria provided (well above the 4 minimum). All three route recalculation triggers enumerated (new delivery BR-4, failed delivery BR-5, manual deviation BR-6).

observations.text: Four well-defined bounded contexts with clear ownership boundaries
10 business rules with thorough invariant justifications — goes well beyond minimum
10 acceptance criteria in proper Given/When/Then format
9 edge cases covering concurrency, empty states, and operational gaps
5 escalations with options and recommendations — excellent cross-context dependency surfacing for MJ
Rich ubiquitous language with 12 defined terms

Route recalculation triggers (BR-4, BR-5, BR-6) all marked invariant:false — a reasonable argument exists that these should be invariant:true since the spec uses MUST language. However, Bird's justification for false is defensible (they're policy choices, not physical constraints)
Cross-context dependency between delivery lifecycle and route recalculation explicitly identified in the multi-driver zone escalation and edge cases
Confidence level of 52 is well-calibrated given the genuine ambiguities around multi-driver zones and shift-end behavior

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: 4 bounded contexts identified (Route Optimization, Fleet Management, Delivery, Customer Notification). Cross-context dependencies surfaced in escalations (multi-driver zone, recalculation triggers crossing context boundaries). Vehicle class rule correctly marked invariant: true (BR-1), stop duration correctly marked invariant: false (BR-8). 9 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided (well above the 4 minimum). All three recalculation triggers (new delivery, failed delivery, manual deviation) correctly enumerated in BR-4/5/6 and the ubiquitous language.

observations.text: Identified 4 distinct bounded contexts with clear ownership boundaries for each
Excellent invariant classification with detailed justifications — BR-1 (vehicle class) correctly true, BR-8 (stop duration) correctly false
9 acceptance criteria covering route assignment, vehicle class filtering, all recalculation triggers, notifications, zone overrides, and edge cases
10 well-thought-out edge cases including concurrent recalculation, shift boundaries, and notification deduplication
Strong escalations identifying the multi-driver zone gap as a critical architectural ambiguity with concrete options
Rich ubiquitous language with 11 terms precisely defined

Cross-context dependencies could be more explicitly framed as integration points for MJ — the bounded_context field mentions 'must integrate' but doesn't formally specify data contracts or event flows between contexts

Route recalculation triggers (BR-4/5/6) marked invariant: false, which is defensible but diverges from the rubric expectation of invariant: true — Bird's justification is reasonable (they're operational policies) but the expected behavior specified them as invariants

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: 5 bounded contexts identified (Route Planning, Fleet Management, Delivery Management, Customer Notification, Operations Configuration), cross-context dependencies explicitly surfaced in escalations and edge cases (e.g., multi-driver partitioning, recalculation triggers crossing context boundaries), vehicle class constraint correctly marked invariant:true and stop duration correctly marked invariant:false, 9 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided, and all 3 recalculation triggers (new delivery, failed delivery, manual deviation) correctly enumerated.

observations.text: Identified 5 bounded contexts, exceeding the minimum of 3, with clear ownership boundaries for each
9 acceptance criteria with well-structured Given/When/Then format, including edge cases like notification deduplication (AC-6)
Vehicle class constraint (BR-2) correctly classified as invariant:true with strong safety/regulatory justification
Stop duration (BR-7) and notification window (BR-6) correctly classified as invariant:false
Excellent edge case coverage including concurrent recalculation triggers, empty zones, and multi-driver ambiguity
Cross-context dependencies surfaced as formal escalations with options and recommendations for MJ
Rich ubiquitous language glossary with 11 well-defined terms

Route recalculation triggers (BR-3, BR-4, BR-5) marked as invariant:false — the rubric expected invariant:true since the system must always recalculate on these events. Bird's justification (could batch instead) is reasonable but diverges from expected classification
Zone constraint (BR-9) identified as an additional invariant beyond what was expected, showing deeper domain analysis
Business impact analysis is thorough with specific stakeholder impacts and risk assessment
